![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/v6zDEMQs2iI/maxresdefault.jpg?sqp=-oaymwEmCIAKENAF8quKqQMa8AEB-AH-CYAC0AWKAgwIABABGGUgZShlMA8=&rs=AOn4CLB1eU4hXj4ljdPOcUqMF2yw2MpX6g)
# [How I Learned Sales](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v6zDEMQs2iI)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [Hormozi Highlights](http://www.youtube.com/@HormoziHighlight)
**Uploaded at**: 2026-03-04T19:00:53-08:00
**Published at**: 2026-03-04T19:00:53-08:00
**Length**: 11:26
**Views**: 8451
**Likes**: 371
**Category**: People & Blogs

## Description

```
Download your free scaling roadmap here: https://www.acquisition.com/roadmap-yth1
Business owners: Want to scale faster? We provide in-person advisory for companies doing at least $1M per year: http://www.acquisition.com/workshop-yth 



If you’re new to my channel, my name is Alex Hormozi. I’m the founder and managing partner of Acquisition.com. It’s a family office, which is just a formal way of saying we invest our own money into companies. Our 10 portfolio companies bring in over $250,000,000+ per year. Our ownership stake varies between 20% and 100% of them. Given this is a YT channel, and anyone can claim anything, I’ll give you some stuff you can google to verify below.

How I got here…

21: Graduated Vanderbilt in 3 years Magna Cum Laude, and took a fancy consulting job.
23 yrs old: Left my fancy consulting job to start a business (a gym).
24 yrs old: Opened 5 gym locations.
26 yrs old: Closed down 6th gym. Lost everything. 
26 yrs old: Got back to launching gyms (launched 33). Then, lost everything for a 2nd time. 
26 yrs old: In desperation, started licensing model as a hail mary. It worked. 
27 yrs old: "Gym Launch" does $3M profit the next 6 months. Then $17M profit next 12 months.
28 yrs old: Started Prestige Labs. $20M the first year.  
29 yrs old: Launched ALAN, a software company for agencies to work leads for customers. Scaled to $1.7mmo within 6 months. 
31 yrs old: Sold 75% of UseAlan to a strategic buyer in an all stock deal. 
31 yrs old: Sold 66% of Gym Launch & Prestige Labs at $46.2M valuation in all-cash deal to American Pacific Group. (you can google it)
31 yrs old: Started our family office Acquisition.com. We invest and scale companies using the $42M in distributions we had taken + the cash from the $46.2M exit.
32 yrs old: Started making free content showing how we grow companies to make real business education accessible to everyone (and) to attract business owners to invest or scale their businesses. 
34 yrs old: I became co-owner of https://Skool.com to help the many people who want to start a business online do so.

Today: Our portfolio now does $200M/yr between 10 companies. The largest doing $100M/yr the smallest doing $5M per year. Our ownership varies between 20% and 100% ownership of the companies. Many of them we invested in early and helped grow (which is how we make our money - not youtube videos).

To all the gladiators in the arena, we’re all in the middle of writing our own stories. The worse the monsters, the more epic the story.

You either get an epic outcome or an epic story. Both mean you win. 

Keep crushing. May your desires be greater than your obstacles. 

Never quit,

Alex

DISCLOSURE 
Information shared here is for educational purposes only.  Individuals and business owners should evaluate their own business strategies, and identify any potential risks.  The information shared here is not a guarantee of success. Your results may vary.
Copyright © 2026.
```

## Transcript

Now, when I watch you, bro, your stuff, it's so it makes so much sense and it's so digestible. You'll hear a lot of sales trainers and it all sounds the same. My first question is this. Where the did you study sales, bro? Like, who was it from? Where where did it start? Who did you was it Brian Tracy? Where did you learn from? So, really interesting. Um, I actually only learned about the sales world after I'd already done 4,000 sales. And so, I looked and gym launch. That was in my gyms. So, I started as a brick and mortar gym owner. And so I remember the first time I was like, it's actually funny because I graduated from college and one of my dad's friends was like, "Hey, you should work at my office." He was a a wealth manager. Yeah. And he was like, "You'd work in sales." And I was like, "I'm not a salesman. I'm an academic, you know, I'm a I'm a I'm a I'm a student." And he was like, "All right, man. Whatever." And so I I end up getting into fitness and then as soon as I opened my doors, I was like, "Shoot, how am I going to get money?" I was like, "I got to get these strangers to give me money." And then I guess that's when I started realizing I had to sell. And so, um, all of the sales that I learned was literally face-toface in person. So, I was very fortunate that I, uh, learned how to run Facebook ads in 2013. So, really, really early to Facebook ads. And so, I was able to get leads really cheap. And so, for basically like four or five years, I took 20 inerson consults a day. Um, and so I got a lot of reps. And so, what ended up happening is I started developing my my team is that I had more and more locations. And I closed at such a high percentage also just because I got more reps than anybody else that they would just start stacking. They would stack like a week's worth of sales for me and I would go to like La Habra one day and do 25 sales and then I would go to my, you know, Lam Marada location, do 25 sales. Then I go to Huntington Beach and I do another 25 sales. And so I And you weren't training at this time. You were just doing them. Just learning. Yeah. Just selling. Yeah. Just selling. But you weren't you weren't like listening to sales training or anything like that. I didn't even know that world existed. I didn't know the whole world existed at all. I only found out about it when I wanted to scale my sales team. So, I had six gyms. I sold those and then I started doing gym turnarounds. So, I started because I was really good at marketing and sales for the gyms. And so, I was like, man, I would fill my gyms up so fast that I was like, I honestly just need gyms to fill up so I can do more sales, right? And so, then it was like, well, I'll just find empty gyms and say, hey, let me sit at the front door. I'll fill it up. And that way, I had all this capacity I could sell into. And we would get 30 to1 on our ad spend. And so, I'd spend a grand or spend 10 grand and make a lot of money in 30 days. And so I would average about 100 grand in cash collected in 21 days of me just sitting at the front of a gym. And I did 33 turnarounds over two years just basically doing that model. And so it was just like Alex with a fold out table and pens and contracts and I would just hold court and I would set as many of point I'd set three appointments an hour um or every 30 minutes and you know two out of three would show and sometimes three out of three would show and sometimes one out of three would show and I would just do the same pitch over and over and over again and so um I'm sure that you like once you start you start breathing the script more than you start knowing it and I think you start realizing how people work and my what was interesting is that the sales training that I had when I started doing the turnarounds because then I was like, you know what, if I can do one of these, if I had 10 guys, then we could do 10 turnarounds a month. Like that'd be crazy. And so I scaled the sales team up to eight guys at the time and each of those guys was now taking 20 appointments a day at different gyms across the country. Got it. And the sales training that I had for those guys was like 60 minutes, the whole thing, right? And it was because it wasn't like I was trying to sell sales training. I just needed them to close. And so it was just like when the person walks in the door, this is what you say. You say, "Hey, you want to see the world's shortest tour?" And then you walk into the gym and you turn around because they don't care about the tour anyways. And they get a laugh. And then you're like, "Awesome. So what brought you in today? What's the thing you're struggling with?" And so what would happen is is like right off the bat if someone said something like, "H, you know, I just wanted to find out more information." I'm like, "Oh, you just opt into lots of different stuff, get information, or you're trying to find, you know, solve a problem." I was like, "I'm assuming you're just not trying to get information for lots of things." They're like, "Oh, well, you know, I'm trying." But the thing is, if you don't confront that, then later they're like, "Well, I just wanted to find out." But if you get them to admit the problem, then it's like we can move forward in the sale. And so, basically, at every one of the six or seven key questions that we had in the sale, it's like, "Here are the four or five offshoots that people are going to throw at you that we need to make sure that we handle, right?" And so, and when you mean offshoots, you mean objections. Yeah. Or Yeah. Exactly. Got it. And so, I just want to make sure that people were handling handling those things because it's like we have had this one conversation more times than anyone else has. There is no way they should be able to say anything that we have not heard before. So, you have no excuse to be unprepared for anything that Susie says when she comes in the door. She's going to say she has to think about it. She's not sure if these workouts are right for her. What if it doesn't work for her body type? She has low thyroid. She has to talk to her husband. She has to look at a couple other gyms. She wants to have time to make the decision. She doesn't have the card she wants to use. It's a little bit too much. Like, there's not that many things, right? There's not that many things that she's going to bring up. And so, we just need to know how we can thoughtfully walk through that without seeming like hardcore sales. And so like my whole objective was to teach people to close, not to sell sales training. And so all the stuff that I've had that I talk about now is just the stuff that I use to train my team. And so right now a lot of our sales trainings are actually very very short. And the most of the effort like if we buy a company, so we just we just um bought a company and we we added a 40 person sales team to went from zero to 40 people in 90 days um taking basically ascension calls. So that that company had an automated sales process on the front end selling um like kind of e-commerce thing and I was like man if we just called all those people and just sold them something else it' probably make us more money and it did and so the idea was like okay well how do we look at the script and then how do we just compress it and compress it and compress it and just make it as short as possible. Um and so we try to delete more than anything else that we possibly can because at least in my belief there's this tiny clip from Y Combinator where the founder of Base Camp talks about when he sold shoes. Mind if I tell the story? I think it's really cool. Yeah. So he says, "Hey, when I sold shoes, you know, the the shoe reps from Wilson, it was a tennis store, Wilson and and Nike or whatever would come and they'd say, "Hey, you know, these are the new graphite bottom, rubber, extra light aerosol, and they tell them all the specs about the shoe." He's like, "But you know, people how people actually buy shoes." He said, "They come in, they'd put the shoe on, they'd walk back and forth for a little bit, and then do this little bouncy thing, and then they'd kind of like look at their shoe in the mirror, and then they'd come to me and they'd ask me how much it was." And he's like, "So all they really want to know is it comfortable? Does it look good? And can I afford it?" Right? And that was it. And I found that for the most part in sales, like there's not a ton of things that actually drive the purchase. And most of the time sales people talk themselves out of sales rather than just closing. And we study this because we we do a lot of analytics on the sales teams that we have across all the portfolio is you I have this belief that every time you make a statement, you give something you give the other person something to disagree with. And so you're like smoke if you just simply ask questions. Even when somebody has an objection, right? And they say, "Hey, you know, like I'll give you a gym example." It's like, "Well, um, are your trainer certified?" I'd be like, "What what certification you looking for?" And then they'd be like, uh, because they don't know, right? They just think they have to ask that question to see to seem like an informed consumer. Correct. And so or like, well, what would you what would what would the certification accomplish for you? And so just literally asking questions about their objection usually would just sidestep the objection more times than not, right? And so that's what I try and train the guys is like if you say nothing, there's nothing anyone can disagree with you on. And we just because they will only agree with themselves. We can't sell them. They have to agree. Like they have to they have to say, you know what, this makes sense. And so we get them to say that by asking them the question and that's that's pretty much the methodology that we've worked with. And you know, I I taught something that I called the closer framework for a very long time because it's just simple and because people can learn it. And so like we have a big chain of of teeth whitening studios. I have to teach someone who makes $10 an hour to sell a thousand dollar thing and sell like many of them every day. So, how do I do that? Like that's the challenge that I like to take on is like, how can I make this process so easy? Because in my opinion, the higher ticket sales most of the time sales training is very vague because the people who do the training and I know we're going to talk about this don't know what they're doing at all. But I believe that if you can really systematize the process, anyone can sell and they just follow kind of color by numbers. And so the closer framework is simply clarify why someone's there. Label them with a problem that you can solve. Overview their past experiences, sell the vacation, which I usually have is three main points. Almost every pitch I it's like, okay, you want this, this, and this. You want fitness, nutrition, accountability, you want the leads to be timely, exclusive, and whatever. So when you mean the vacation, it's like their dream. Yes. What they want. Exactly. It's and the the AC that that little story comes from uh a lot of times people sell the plane flight, not the vacation. They're like you got to go through TSA, you got to bring your bags and this is what we're going to do on the flight and here's the check. That's like no no no, no one cares. They won't talk about Maui. We'll talk about the vacation. Like let's talk about that vacation. And then E is explaining away their concerns and R is reinforce the decision. So that's the closer framework. That way you know someone's like, "Okay, I'll do it." And then it's like now this was a great decision. Here are the next steps and let's walk you through what it's going to be like to accomplish this and let me tell you exactly what happens next. because that's where people get cold feet, they back out. You get chargebacks, you get refunds, things like that. It's like we need to have a clear handshake between the sale and whatever the next step they have to do and it's just smooth process, right? And so we link those. You're cuz I I think the same way where you don't need the most craziest closing line to close somebody because it I think it starts in the beginning to me. I think you could [ __ ] everything up within the first 5 seconds of beginning. And when people have these I was we were just on a show like 10 minutes ago. He's like, "Man, you know what's the secret closing line?" And I never even thought of sales like that because I said at the end of I've never used some wicked closing line where I turn back to my guys in the office. I'm like, "Did you [ __ ] hear that line?" And I'm like the, you know, I'm like the in-your-face sales guy, you know, very very outgoing. But I never had one line that closed somebody. And it's always been so easy towards the end because it's everything leading up to the sale where you shouldn't even try to attempt for the close unless you intimately know that you have the clothes. Dude, I say that all the time. It's like lawyers don't ask questions they don't already know the answer to. Correct. like I don't ask for the unless I know they're going to say yes. And so like the more I think that's where that's where I do think some pattern recognition with sales like you're like I don't think they're there yet. You know what I mean? Like what's what else are you concerned about? Like what are you really worried about right now? And I think that's where like the the asking genuine questions. And I think one of the things that I got from Ila is keeping the human first. I know that sounds kind of soft, but like actually having that as the frame because I genuinely believe the person who cares more about the prospect wins. M and so like if they care basically in that moment if you care more about them than they do over the long term you will be able to convince them because you actually are putting their needs first and that also means that sometimes and I tell my guys this if you get to the end of the conversation you really don't think it's the best thing for them to do push away. Yeah. And that's a successful sale to me. Yeah. Because my point the end point of a sale for me is a decision not a sale. That's how I see it. And that way my guys can win on every single conversation. I just want to get to aision a decision one way or another. Real quick, if you're a business owner and you are not growing as fast as you'd like, I'd like to give you a free gift. So, my team and I put together the $100 million scaling roadmap, which is basically 200 hours of us looking over all the portfolio companies we've had and what stages of growth they went through and more importantly where they got stuck and how they got past it. And so we broke it into these 10 stages and we made this little kind of quiz thing where if you put in your business information, it'll tell you where you're at and the most important part for you, what to do for each of functions of the business across product, marketing, sales, customer success, recruiting, IT, human resources, and finance. And so no matter what you're struggling with, someone else has already struggled with it and solved it. And so I'd like to give you this thing absolutely free. You can go to acquisition.com/roadmap, plug in your business information, and if you want us to actually help you deconstrain the business and you're trying to scale, we'd love to help you out on the thank you page. You can just book a call with my team and we will look at the business, see if we can help, and if we can, we'll invite you out to Vegas and we'll do this in person live.

If you're new to my channel, my name is Alex Hormozi. I'm the founder and managing partner of Acquisition.com. It's a family office, which is just a formal way of saying we invest our own money into companies. Our 10 portfolio companies bring in over $250,000,000+ per year. Our ownership stake varies between 20% and 100% of them. Given this is a YT channel, and anyone can claim anything, I'll give you some stuff you can google to verify below.

How I got here…

21: Graduated Vanderbilt in 3 years Magna Cum Laude, and took a fancy consulting job.
23 yrs old: Left my fancy consulting job to start a business (a gym).
24 yrs old: Opened 5 gym locations.
26 yrs old: Closed down 6th gym. Lost everything.
26 yrs old: Got back to launching gyms (launched 33). Then, lost everything for a 2nd time.
26 yrs old: In desperation, started licensing model as a hail mary. It worked.
27 yrs old: "Gym Launch" does $3M profit the next 6 months. Then $17M profit next 12 months.
28 yrs old: Started Prestige Labs. $20M the first year.
29 yrs old: Launched ALAN, a software company for agencies to work leads for customers. Scaled to $1.7mmo within 6 months.
31 yrs old: Sold 75% of UseAlan to a strategic buyer in an all stock deal.
31 yrs old: Sold 66% of Gym Launch & Prestige Labs at $46.2M valuation in all-cash deal to American Pacific Group. (you can google it)
31 yrs old: Started our family office Acquisition.com. We invest and scale companies using the $42M in distributions we had taken + the cash from the $46.2M exit.
32 yrs old: Started making free content showing how we grow companies to make real business education accessible to everyone (and) to attract business owners to invest or scale their businesses.
34 yrs old: I became co-owner of [https://Skool.com](https://www.youtube.com/redirect?event=video_description&redir_token=QUFFLUhqa1hISjZ2eTduenZWb0dqZE5KSkNwV01MTHB6QXxBQ3Jtc0tuQXlnYktMV0QwNmVBQVdjWk5kWVNncF9pNG80cWwzemFXOFl6ZVliRjlpYmtBQ1R6UUZSUHkzQ0NONUY0NlFmVm9ZeDh3Q3d5em5TUXE4V3hUVXBnM0dXSWx4QzhJN1RDMFl3eFo3MG8ybEpoRGluWQ&q=https%3A%2F%2FSkool.com%2F), which is a platform for people to build communities online, making a living doing what they love, with people like them.
36 yrs old: I did a $106M book launch selling 3.6M copies of my $100M Money Models book, in 72 hours, breaking the Guinness world record for the fastest selling non-fiction book of all time.

Today: Our portfolio now does $200M/yr between 10 companies. The largest doing $100M/yr the smallest doing $5M per year. Our ownership varies between 20% and 100% ownership of the companies. Many of them we invested in early and helped grow (which is how we make our money - not youtube videos).

To all the gladiators in the arena, we're all in the middle of writing our own stories. The worse the monsters, the more epic the story.

You either get an epic outcome or an epic story. Both mean you win.

Keep crushing. May your desires be greater than your obstacles.

Never quit,

Alex

web.markdown:
![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/_-3gbRE2BnU/maxresdefault.jpg)
# [Alex Hormozi's Advice On Sales Funnels](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_-3gbRE2BnU)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [ThriveCart](http://www.youtube.com/@ThriveCartPlatform)
**Uploaded at**: 2025-05-15T17:00:40-07:00
**Published at**: 2025-05-15T17:00:40-07:00
**Length**: 13:02
**Views**: 508
**Likes**: 12
**Category**: People & Blogs

## Description

```
Alex Hormozi's Advice On Sales Funnels
Get lifetime access - no more shopping cart or recurring monthly fees: https://thrivecart.com/company/youtube/

I break down everything I’ve learned from Alex Hormozi and share the best Alex Hormozi business tips to help you master your own strategy. Whether you’re diving into business tips for the first time or are a seasoned entrepreneur, this video delivers actionable advice based on Hormozi's approach.

I also dive into key lessons from a recent Alex Hormozi interview, touching on Alex Hormozi sales, the full Alex Hormozi sales system, and proven Alex Hormozi sales training techniques. This is essential sales training that will help you understand how to sell in today's market. I cover sales tips and how to build a high-converting sales funnel, answer “what is a sales funnel”, and explain step-by-step how to build a sales funnel.

We’ll also get into sales funnel marketing, effective sales funnel strategy, and managing successful sales funnels through proper sales funnel management. I’ll show you how conversion rate optimization (also known as CRO) plays a vital role in scaling your results. If you're in sales, you need to sharpen your sales techniques and build powerful sales skills—and this video will help you do just that.
```

## Transcript

She says this and I was like, "How are you doing this?" And she's like, "You know what? I got my first sale when I had eight people on my email list." Hands down, Alexi has created some of the greatest sales funnels to ever exist. There is no way he could have grown his company Jim Launch to $46 million exit and build a portfolio of over 16 companies if he didn't get crazy good at the skill of building sales funnels. So, in this video, I'm going to be revealing the strategy Alex Hermosia has used to create absolutely killer funnels so you can seal his learnings and techniques and apply them to your own business. We secret shopped the business. So, we actually looked into it. We went there. By the way, you should secret shop your own business. Highly recommend doing it. You'll be horrified by what you hear your sales guy say. You're like, I thought we had a script. What are you even doing? This is an extremely valuable point because a lot of times we forget about such glaringly obvious mistakes that we could be making. Get into the mindset of your client where they don't know what you're thinking. They don't know your background. They don't know anything about you. And assume that they're just coming in as a blank slate. Go through every single part of your funnel and start looking for anything that might not be super clear, anything that seems like it's off, anything that's missing or anything that's literally broken. And you'll probably find a lot of things to fix. I asked him, so I went through this checklist. I was like, "Okay, so did they give you some sort of pre-sale question?" He was like, "No." I was like, "Okay, great. did they ask you for your credit card or did they ask you for your information or anything before you got the service? And he was like, "No." And I was like, "Fantastic." Did they have you pick where you're at, what your goal is? He said, "Yeah, they did have me pick where I was at, but not where I wanted to go." So, they just had him pick, "Okay, how ugly are you?" How I see this applying to funnels a lot of times is I see people going with either just one angle on their sales pages or their funnels, and they're forgetting about the fact that human beings are multi-dimensional. And being able to address not only what does the person actually want, but what are they struggling with? what are they willing to pay for? This is just a great example of remembering to come back to who exactly is a person that's buying and incorporating that as you're actually creating your funnels. And then they say, "Oh, well, we have payment plans that we have interest on. You do have 10% interest if you can put half down and we have 20% interest if you put nothing down and you just go on to a straight payment plan." Well, which one would you rather buy? point is is that this is how you present the price because you get the benefit of a price anchor and you get to be the good guy for getting them to pay upfront rather than the bad guy for them not being able to. I absolutely love this point. This is one of the biggest shifts that I made in my business. So, for context, I've been in this particular coaching business for 11 years and I've been coaching entrepreneurs on funnels and business strategies. And this is the biggest thing that I always tell them to do because when it comes to presenting the price point, pay in full or payment plan. And the payment plan is always more expensive. So people always think about the interest. Being able to shift away from thinking about anything around how much extra you're going to have to pay, but instead looking at what is the discount for paying in full changes the game entirely. Now you're the good guy. Now you're the person that's like, I'm here to support you. I'm going to reward you for paying in full. I get a lot more people paying in full since I implemented this shift and it's been a few years. A really easy way to navigate this is by creating a checkout page that lists all the options so people can make a decision really quickly. For example, when you're inside of Thrive Kart, you're able to set various different pricing options. I always start with the pay in full discount. Let's say it's uh $1,997. And the way that I say it, you can actually name it separately. So instead of just onetime payment, and here's what it is, I say something like pay in full, say $500. And then you can set up the actual payment plans. So you can do a subscription, split pay, however makes sense for your business based on what the total price is that you would like people to pay without the discount. A friend of mine has a recurring membership that he sells. I think he was selling at 300 bucks a month and he couldn't get people to stick past 3 months. He tried all these different gimmicks and things and he just couldn't crack 3 months of LTV. Now that could have been a pricing issue, whatever. He stopped selling it at $300 a month, monthtomonth, and started selling us a $10,000 program with 36 months of interest free financing. And so when people bought, they were buying at $10,000 price point, but they got an amazing payment plan. He didn't change anything about what he sold, but that took his average customer from 3 months to 8 months. I actually tell my clients this all the time. Now, I never judge based on what offer you might want to sell. Some of my clients like to sell memberships. That's totally fine. I've sold memberships and I still do have month-to-month memberships, but I always see the massive lifetime value difference between somebody who's signing up for a bigger program where they're signing up for a payment plan, say over 6 months, 12 months, or even longer, versus somebody that's committing to a month-to-month payment plan. The problem with a recurring month-to-month subscription is that every single month you basically have to resell this person on should I stay, should I leave? Like there's always a decision that has to be made. And even when somebody has gone through a few months, because a lot of times with memberships, if you think about gyms, we sign up for a gym and then we don't go for like a year and it's like, well, I should probably cancel and you feel bad for not going. So that mindset is no longer an issue when you sell them a fullon whatever type of program that you're selling. let's say a six-month access or 12-month access or even lifetime access and then they're simply paying off the payment. It actually removes a lot of hesitation and customer service issues from people because everyone's on the same page and expectations are super clear and set from the very beginning. And on transactional side, you've got like high velocity sales. We're talking 20 30 minute sales. A guy who stands in front of the car wash sells car wash. Somebody sells gym memberships. Transactional. And then you've kind of got like this middle where you might sell something that's a little bit higher ticket but it's a little bit more custom. I took their sale from here, a purely transactional sale, and moved it towards custom. Now, the advantages of having this type of sale is that it's really fast. They tend to be lower ticket in general. And from an operational perspective in the business, you don't need to personalize anything. And so, you get the sales team and the sales process to basically orient everyone like a funnel down to one solution. And then you just make a ton of these solutions and you get lots of efficiencies because you only have to produce one thing. The custom sale is a easier sale to do, but harder on the operational side. And so the magic happens when you can actually bridge the product component of transactional, which you say, okay, we only sell these types of widgets. This is the only thing we deliver, but I can do it in a way that feels custom, feels personalized. I kind of look at it as transactional funnels versus more of those custom funnels that you can take people through. So a lot of times the transactional funnels are more low ticket, one sizefits-all. I have a lot of them in my business as well, and they're really great for generating customers for a pretty low cost, especially if you're using paid ads. But then you don't want to only rely on transactional funnels because ultimately there's going to be people who join your audience who realize that they actually want something more. They want feedback. They want guidance. And I find that especially nowadays with AI, everyone's getting feedback from ChatGBT. People are really valuing human connection and human support and knowing that what they're creating is going to be better and more customized to their situation, which is where those custom funnels can come in. So, you can sell a low ticket offer with a transactional funnel. Move people then into a custom funnel where you can move people into a consulting program, a group coaching program. Something that I tell my clients to do is we create a group coaching program that's just a group offer with some community support, but you actually can offer a VIP tier where they can upgrade for an extra th000 or $2,000 where they get actual feedback on their meal plan or what they're building. For me, it's like, I'll review your funnel. I'll review your copy and stuff like that. And it's a very easy upgrade because people value that personalization. She had 5,800 followers. Her average post got 19 likes and like one comment. And 100% of her acquisition came from her organic Instagram. She's doing 10 plus sales a day at a low ticket price and doing threeish sales a week at a higher ticket price. And when I say higher ticket, I'm talking like $45,000. She says this and I was like, "How are you doing this?" And she was like, "You know what? I got my first sale when I had eight people on my email list." And I was like, "That's nuts." And she was like, "Yeah." So it was really confirming for me that like there people were listening. This is the power of having a hypers specific niche where you're able to create specific solutions, products, coaching, programs for a very specific subset of a population that has a very unique problem where maybe not a lot of people are talking about it. Maybe there isn't quite as much competition on it yet. But even if there's competition, don't get discouraged because really it doesn't take a lot of people. As you saw in this example, you can have a very, very small audience, but if your offer clearly communicates to the right person and you're speaking to their unique problem, you'll get the sale. And we see this with this example, but we see it with a lot of people, too, which is why it's so powerful to have hypersp specific offers, hyperspecific messaging instead of going super broad, which is what most people are tempted to do. Most people want to just focus on a really broad topic like just weight loss or just fitness or just business. And the more you can be specific, the more you're going to speak to a subset of a population that is looking for that specific solution because they already resonate with it and they're just looking for someone like you to purchase from. You only need a,000 true fans. You can do whatever you want with a,000 fans even at 10 bucks a month, right? Like you can make $100,000 a year if you just do that, right? A thousand true fans that are die hard and you can make a lot more than that with a thousand true fans. And so people think because we have this 8 billion people in the world that you need to be Kim Kardashian, you need to be Mr. Beast and sure with massive numbers like those like you're going to get famous. Those things are required. But like you can make an absolute killing just talking about what you're really good at. The idea here is that tiny engaged audiences tend to convert way better than massive audiences where they're passive and they're only following you just for the entertainment value. Anytime that you have an offer that you're able to provide a specific solution to a specific problem, like he talked about, your funnel is going to perform a lot better. This also gives you a great opportunity to create additional bumps and upsells within the salesunnel knowing that you can address very specific problems within the circumstance that this person is facing. So, this gives you the ability to move into having recurring revenue like memberships or group programs or consulting where people are paying a payment plan or an actual month-to-month fee. It kind of depends on your preference. You don't need as many clients as you need in order to make really solid income and be able to if you're still in a 9 toive job to be able to quit that. and being able to move people down the funnel to enrolling into those future offers. Very specific content, very specific ads, whether it's posting organically, like one post a day on Instagram or Tik Tok, allows you to position yourself as a go-to expert as long as you're speaking to those problems and not just focusing on how do I go viral. Let's focus on solving those problems. Your business metrics don't follow your vanity metrics there. And so just because you get more views doesn't mean you get more revenue. Just because you get more subs doesn't mean you get more opt-ins for your website or for whatever it is that you sell. You don't get sales from that stuff. Subscribers don't mean sales. Don't be afraid of the empty room. Very low views doesn't mean low revenue. It just means that it's more specific and that you're talking to a more specific person. And a specific problem being solved for a specific person is how you make big money. I tell this all the time because I am not a huge organic marketing person. I really focus on what is going to get me opt-ins, what is going to get me sales. And that's the way that you want to think about it, too. Especially if you're wanting to go allin on sales funnels. And why wouldn't you want to when you can start going from like one customer that was going to pay you say $17. You can turn that customer into 100, $200, $300 within the same transaction. That's the power of having a salesunnel. But then secondary, being able to see, okay, from every person who sees my page, how many of them are opting in? How many of them are purchasing my low ticket offer? How many of those buyers are then upgrading to my order bump to my upsell to another offer that I'm presenting afterwards after they've started consuming that initial offer that they've purchased. This is the power. It's looking at the numbers. It's looking at data and being able to optimize for how do I make this better? And I know there's a lot of people that don't want to have a niche, but the more you are specific and the more you have a niche, the easier it is for you to attract the right people, the buyers. Because even if you have a lot of people following you, doesn't mean that they're going to actually buy your offer. if they're coming to you for just pure entertainment value or just to learn about your story or just about a lot of different random things that you talk about. When you are more specific and you're targeting buyers, you're speaking to buyer problems, you're speaking to buyer solutions, you're attracting buyers who obviously are going to buy either immediately or eventually from consuming your content. Platforms reward niche content. They know who the right person is and they start to serve your content to those right people instead of having you be all over the place and having a bunch of random people see your stuff and following you because of your looks or because of anything else that's not actually relevant to the real data and the real metrics that drive results. The biggest thing that jumped out of me through all of this advice that we just heard is that it's never been easier to get into online business. But it's not just the fact that you can make an absolute killing with a small following that's making it easy. There's four main reasons that online businesses are in easy mode right now. So, click here to check out the next video where I explain those four reasons, what they actually mean for you, and how you can take advantage of the easiest time in history to start an online business and start making money. If you want to easily grow a business online, I'll see you over

web.markdown:
![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/9of8jiyYBOI/maxresdefault.jpg)
# [Alex Hormozi’s $100M Advice for B2B Services](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9of8jiyYBOI)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [Jordan Rafealov](http://www.youtube.com/@JordanRafealov)
**Uploaded at**: 2026-01-14T03:23:54-08:00
**Published at**: 2026-01-14T03:23:54-08:00
**Length**: 32:50
**Views**: 631
**Likes**: 16
**Category**: Education

## Description

```
👉 Get your FREE Growth Plan: https://dopaminedigital.io/
👉  Join The Skool: https://www.skool.com/shipitsociety/about?ref=d6335ef7d94b4e6ab557026fcdd8771b

This video breaks down how to apply Alex Hormozi's strategies effectively for B2B service-based businesses. We emphasize that "business to business" buyers prioritize avoiding job loss over emotional decisions, so your "marketing strategy" and messaging around that. Learn how to build AI growth engines and use "ai for b2b" to scale, with a guaranteed pipeline increase within 90 days for your "b2b sales" efforts, or you don't pay. This focuses on practical "b2b marketing" techniques for real-world results.
```

## Transcript

If you are a B2B service-based business, this is the video for you because most hormosi content is applied completely wrong in the B2B space because B2B buyers do not buy emotionally. They do not decide alone and they don't move fast just for fun. All they care about is not getting fired. And so you need to orchestrate all of your messaging and your offer around that. So, I'm going to be talking about how Horoszi would break down your business if you were a B2B service-based business. And then right at the end, I'm also going to show you what it would be like if Horoszi himself put together the offers, the guarantee, and the strategy for five different industries in the service-based business. The first thing that you need to understand is that B2B buyers do not buy deliverables. If you are selling audits, workshops, reports, they don't improve KPIs. KPIs are really important for a B2B business. And so you need to be selling outcomes. Stop selling deliverables. It's a race to the bottom on pricing. And with long sales cycles and no guarantees, you will get crushed. Because clients don't see an ROI. All they see is just work done. and then they start comparing you to the freelancer who charges $6 an hour and then you charge them 10 grand. Which one is going to be the one that they choose? So ask yourself first, are you selling a series of deliverables or are you selling outcomes? And there is some nuance here because the outcome needs to be a uh enormous amount of value. They need to communicate the dream outcome. You need to communicate the perceived likelihood of them achieving that outcome in how long will it take them and how much effort and sacrifice will it take for them to do that. And then you can also list line by line the things that they get within that outcome and that offer. So try to sell Maui don't sell the journey but when you onboard them that's when you sell the journey if that makes sense. So dream outcome for B2B is more like pipeline value, churn percent, CAC, payback, etc. And the perceived likelihood of them achieving that outcome is increased with client interviews and case studies. So when a client first comes on board with me, the first thing that I do is I ask them, who was your most successful customer? Can we beg, borrow, and steal 10 minutes with them to record a case study? and why were they the best customer? Do you love working with them? Are they the perfect fit? Are they an anomaly? Is there anybody else like that that we can go to and we can also record their successes? Now, if we can, what you will find is that they will all say similar things and then we create the ICP and the based on what worked for them really well and who they are because I guarantee you there are thousands of people just like them that exist in this universe that you can go to and you can sell your thing to. Why are you wasting your time time trying to sell something to somebody that isn't that exact ICP? And speaking of ICP, the big mistake that people make here in the B2B servicebased space is that they go after an industry, not a person. So instead of going I service SAS or I go after agencies or I'm a B2B uh company provider, you need to be targeting the people who are paying the invoice that they care about this metric and they feel the pain personally. So this could be the CFO, it could be the COO, it could be the CRO, whoever is the person who signs your check is the one that you need to own the relationship with. And sell to the owner of the problem, not the category of the company. So let me say that again. Sell to the owner of the problem, not the category of the company. and how you put together a grand slam offer in the B2B space is similar, but there is some nuance. And again, I'm going to pump out five different offers across five different industries and ICPs. The goal here is to make the offer so good, saying no feels ridiculous, like it feels irrational. It's not an emotional decision. It should feel irrational. So like we discussed you need to uh identify the hypersp specific ICP. So you can say instead of SAS companies mid-market B2B SAS companies with 50 to 500 employees with 15 plus% churn and you target the CEO and founders specifically. Now what are you going to be promising? Like what is the KPI that they can measure because they are going to have people on their board. They're going to have people in their company that they are going to need to go to with your proposal and convince them given all the assets that you've given and all the arguments that you've given why they should allocate tens of thousands of dollars to your thing and why they're choosing you as opposed to getting a freelancer in or they are getting some person inhouse, right? Which a lot of people think is going to be more affordable. So if you just come in and you say you're a consultant, right? Let's say you say we're going to help you improve your sales process. Where is the measurable KPI that you can judge success against? If we say we generate $2 million in pipeline value within 90 days, that's measurable. That's very easy to understand. ## Description

```
Get ready for the most valuable sales conversation of the 21st century as two of the world's greatest sales legends sit-down and give a complete masterclass in sales. 

Daniel G dig deeps with Alex Hormozi discussing every part of his sales cycle, from recruiting great sales talent to an impeccable closer frame work. 

Are you looking to take your sales game to the next level? Join Daniel G's new sales role play community hosted in Skool to master every single part of  your sales business. Click the link below to join today! 

https://www.skool.com/world-class-sales-role-play-2533/about

Connect with Daniel G and Alex Hormozi on Social at: 

www.instagram.com/DanielG
www.instagram.com/Hormozi

Don's forget to subscribe to this new channel if you want more sales content like this! 

---
```

And I'm sure they do this with long form content as well. He's going to sit down and he's just going to batch shoot. Instead of shoot one video, he's going to knock out four. He's going to knock out five or six. That way, when you're in the mode of presenting, when you're in the mode of shooting content, you can just keep going with it, keep going with it. And that's a really good way to kind of get ahead. It's something that Alex does in his content. And then another thing is retakes. And so they actually uh he said he shot a ton of retakes for the intro of this video. Uh I'll show you this right here. And this video right here, we did eight different takes of the intro where I slowly worked from 30 seconds down to eight and then that's what became the intro that you saw before seeing this moment right now. That's crazy, right? To shoot a ton of different takes for your intro. But it makes sense because the the intros are so valuable. The way I do this though is like I I write out my hook, my intro, and then what I do is I just keep rewriting it and rewriting it until I get it shorter and shorter and better. And then even with this video when I was saying it to the camera out loud, it actually was like, "Oh, I could take that part out. Oh, I could change this. I could move this around." And then I just refined it. But I spent a lot of my time like I maybe shot my intro like three times, but I'd even like finished the first two because I was like, "Ah, that's too long." And then I'd cut it out, change the script, do it again. And so whether you want to script it and get it really refined, or you just want to do it at the end of your video and reshoot that intro, you can do it either way. Uh, it seems like Alex likes to just reshoot his intros at the very end once he's kind of given all the content. Visuals when you're shooting, if you want to do it like Alex or Mosie, he likes to have different visuals. So, you can see him drawing on paper a lot of the time. I like to draw on my iPad and eventually I'll have a video on how to do that. I'll have a link in the description for that. But, it just makes it easier to follow along for the viewer. And so, I really think this is a great thing that you can do if you have some sort of visual element. uh it's just when you're teaching it's just going to make it easier to follow along. And then he loves to use stories and examples. So when he's making a point, he'll either share a story from himself or maybe working with someone else or he'll just give different examples to make his point. And so in your content and when shooting, if you can use those uh these ideas here, this is going to help making the shooting process a bit better. And uh this is the way Alex likes to do his shooting content. Now the fifth thing, this is really important and there's a few things to cover here, but it's editing. how can we actually uh get better editing and ultimately hold people to watch longer through our editing style. And he likes to do simple edits. If you watch his stuff, it's not overly produced. Uh but they do add visuals. So if you don't have visuals when shooting your content, and by the way, visuals could be this. Like I'm screen sharing. This is a visual so you can see what I'm talking about. This would be way more boring if you couldn't see these examples. You can add them in while editing. So here he has a tweet, which is his first point in this video. and he simply just added this in in editing, making it easier to follow along here. Sometimes they'll add in like different text popups uh just to make it easier for people to see what's happening and it makes the learning experience a little bit better. And then with your intros, if you want to do it like Alex, spend more time editing your intro than you do the rest of your videos. Notice the intro of this video and how much more edited it is than the rest of the video. Uh it's kind of it's kind of funny. Check this out. ## Description

```
Most people think sales is about scripts, tactics, or talking better.
That’s why most people fail.

In this video, Alex Hormozi breaks down the brutal truths about sales that most businesses ignore — the real reasons deals don’t close, pipelines don’t scale, and effort doesn’t turn into revenue.

This isn’t feel-good motivation or surface-level advice. It’s a reality check on how sales actually works in the real world. You’ll learn why most sales teams stall, why founders become the bottleneck, and what really separates consistent closers from everyone else.

If you’re struggling to close deals, scale a sales team, or turn interest into revenue, this video will show you what you’re doing wrong — and what actually works.

Watch this video if you want to:

Understand why most sales efforts fail

Learn the core principles behind scalable sales

Stop relying on scripts and start building real leverage

Improve close rates without manipulation

Build a sales system that works without you

These are the truths most people avoid — and pay for.
```
